Question:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The first ionization enthalpy of oxygen is less than that of nitrogen.
Statement II: Nitrogen has a half-filled 2p subshell, which provides extra stability.
Statement III: The atomic radius of nitrogen is greater than that of oxygen.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
Solution:
Statement I — Correct: Oxygen has lower ionization enthalpy than nitrogen due to electron–electron repulsion in paired 2p orbitals.
Statement II — Correct: Nitrogen (2p³) has half-filled configuration → extra stability → higher ionization enthalpy.
Statement III — Incorrect: Atomic radius decreases across a period → Oxygen is smaller than Nitrogen.

Question: Which of the following is not correct about Green Revolution?
Options: It succeeded in tripling the food supply.
Increased yield was insufficient to feed the growing human population.
Increased yields could be attributed to improved crop varieties and use of agrochemicals.
Further increase in yield with existing varieties could be achieved using conventional breeding.
Solution:
The Green Revolution increased food production mainly through high-yielding varieties (HYVs), fertilizers, irrigation, and pesticides.
Further increase in yield cannot be achieved by conventional breeding alone, as existing varieties had already reached their yield potential.

Question: A man wants to reach point B on the opposite bank of a river flowing at a speed as shown in figure. With what minimum speed and in which direction should the man swim relative to water so that he can reach point B? (u is the speed of the stream of the river)
Solution:
Let v be the speed of man in still water. Resultant of v and u should be along AB. Components of vb (absolute velocity of man) along x and y directions are,
$${v_x} = u - v\sin \theta \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{v_y} = v\cos \theta $$
Further, $$\tan {45^ \circ } = \frac{{{v_y}}}{{{v_x}}} = {\text{or}}\,\,1 = \frac{{v\cos \theta }}{{u - v\sin \theta }}$$
$$\therefore \,\,v = \frac{u}{{\sin \theta  + \cos \theta }} = \frac{u}{{\sqrt 2 \sin \left( {\theta  + {{45}^ \circ }} \right)}}$$
v is minimum at, 
$$\theta  + {45^ \circ } = {90^ \circ }\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\theta  = {45^ \circ }\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{v_{\min }} = \frac{u}{{\sqrt 2 }}$$

Direction is 90o+ 45o = 135o with river flow direction

Question: Two bodies begin a free fall from the same height at a time interval of $${\text{N}}\,{\text{s}}$$. If vertical separation between the two bodies is $${\text{1 m}}$$ after $$n$$ second from the start of the first body, then $$n$$ is equal to $(n > N)$
Solution:
Let $${y_1}$$ be the height through which  body 1 falls in n seconds and $${y_2}$$ be the height through which body 2 falls in (n - N) seconds (because body 2 starts after N seconds) then since there starting velocity $$u = 0$$, we can write

$${y_1} = \frac{1}{2}g{n^2},{y_2} = \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {n - N} \right)^2}$$

$$\therefore {y_1} - {y_2} = \frac{1}{2}g\left[ {{n^2} - {{\left( {n - N} \right)}^2}} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{g}{2}\left( {2n - N} \right)N$$             $$\left[ {\because {y_1} - {y_2} = 1} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{1}{{gN}} + \frac{N}{2}$$

Question: Which of the following is the correct set of physical properties of the geometrical isomers?
Options: Dipole: I > II, Boiling point: I > II, Melting point: II > I, Stability: I > II
Dipole: II > I, Boiling point: II > I, Melting point: II > I, Stability: II > I
Dipole: I > II, Boiling point: I > II, Melting point: I > II, Stability: I > II
Dipole: II > I, Boiling point: II > I, Melting point: I > II, Stability: I > II
Solution:
Dipole moment of I is more than II compound having more dipole moment has more boiling point.
In the cis form (I) close packing is better so its melting point and stability is higher then trans form (II)
Hence correct set of physical properties of the geometrical isomers is:
Dipole: I > II, Boiling point: I > II, Melting point: I > II, Stability: I > II

Question: A string on a musical instrument is 50 cm long and its fundamental frequency is 270 Hz. If the desired frequency of 1000 Hz is to be produced, the required length of the string is
Solution:
Fundamental frequency \[ = \frac{v}{{2L}}\]

\[270 = \frac{v}{{2 \times \left( {50} \right)}}\]

\[v = 270 \times 100\,cm/s\]

Now to make frequency 1000 Hz

\[1000 = \frac{v}{{2\left( L \right)}}\]

\[L = \frac{{270 \times 100}}{{2 \times 1000}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow L = 13.5\,cm\]

Question:
Consider the following information:
A species is the basic unit of classification and is defined as the largest group of organisms in which any two individuals of the appropriate sexes can produce fertile offspring, typically by sexual reproduction in a natural environment. Other ways of defining species include their karyotype, DNA sequence, morphology, behaviour or ecological niche. (Source: wikipedia)
The liger is a hybrid offspring of a male lion (Panthera leo) and a female tiger (Panthera tigris) that was created in captivity. Some ligers are fertile and can breed other ligers that do not live very long. Ligers have not yet been spotted in natural environments.
Based on these 2 pieces of information, should the classification of Panthera leo and Panthera tigris as separate species be challenged?
Options: Yes, the classification of lion and tiger as separate species should be challenged because members of two different species are able to interbreed and produce offspring
No, the classification of lion and tiger as separate species cannot be challenged because ligers were bred in captivity and have not been found in natural habitats
Yes, the classification of lion and tiger as separate species is challenged because this impacts biodiversity
There is not enough information to challenge the species classifications
Solution: The members of a species freely interbreed in the wild and produce fertile and robust offsprings that further reproduce. Ligers were created and further mated in captivity. Ligers are not found in natural environments. Therefore, the species classification of lions and tigers as separate still holds and doesn't have to be challenged.

Question:
Assertion: The gametes of threatened species can be preserved in viable and fertile condition for long periods using cryopreservation techniques.
Reason: Eggs can be fertilised in vitro and plants can be propagated using tissue culture methods.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: In recent years ex situ conservation has advanced beyond keeping threatened species in enclosures. Now gametes of threatened species can be preserved in viable and fertile condition for long periods using cryopreservation techniques, eggs can be fertilised in vitro, and plants can be propagated using tissue culture methods. Seeds of different genetic strains of commercially important plants can be kept for long periods in seed banks. So, Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Question:
The phase difference between two waves, represented by
${y_1} = {10^{ - 6}}{\text{sin}}\left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right) + 0.5} \right]m$
${y_2} = {10^{ - 6}}{\text{cos}}\left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right)} \right]m$
where x is expressed in meters and t is expressed in seconds, is approximately
Solution:
\[{y_1} = {10^{ - 6}}\sin \left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right) + 0.5} \right]\]

\[{y_2} = {10^{ - 6}}\cos \left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right)} \right]\]

[Using \[\cos x = \sin \left( {x + \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\]

\[ = {10^{ - 6}}\sin \left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right) + \frac{\pi }{2}} \right]\]

\[ = {10^{ - 6}}\sin \left[ {100t + \left( {x/50} \right) + 1.57} \right]\]

The phase difference \[ = 1.57 - 0.5 = 1.07\]

Question:
Assertion: Lichens can be used as industrial pollution indicators.
Reason: Lichens forced the moth to change to dark-winged or white-winged in industrial areas.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Lichens can be used as industrial pollution indicators. They will not grow in areas that are polluted. Lichens did not force the moth to change to dark-winged or white-winged in industrial areas. These varieties already existed in nature. Before industrialisation set in, thick growth of almost white-coloured lichen covered the trees - in that background the white winged moth survived but the dark-coloured moth was picked out by predators. So, Assertion is true, but Reason is false.

Question: A block of mass 2kg is released from A on the track that is one quadrant of a circle of radius 1m. It slides down the track and reaches B with a speed of 4ms-1 and finally stops at C, at a distance of 3m from B. The work done against the force of friction is
Solution:
Block possess potential energy at point A = mgh = 2 × 10 × 1 = 20 J
This gets converted to maximum kinetic energy at B and finally the block stops at C, after moving against friction.
So the acquired kinetic energy is consumed while moving against friction i.e. work done against friction is 20 J.

Question: Two stones are projected so as to reach the same distance from the point of projection on a horizontal surface. The maximum height reached by one exceeds the other by an amount equal to half the sum of the height attained by them. Then angle of projection of the stone which attains smaller height is

Two masses \[{m_1}\] and \[{m_2}\] are placed at a distance $$L$$ apart. The center of mass of the system is at O, \[{m_1}\] is displaced by $$a$$ towards O and \[{m_2}\] is displaced by $$b$$ away from O. The distance between the new COM and point O is
Solution:
$$d = \frac{{{m_1}\left( O \right) + {m_2}\left( L \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} = \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}L$$

$$d' = \frac{{{m_1}\left( a \right) + {m_2}\left( {L + b} \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

$$d' = \frac{{{m_1}a + {m_2}L + {m_2}b}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

Now, $$OO' = d' - d = \frac{{{m_1}a + {m_2}L + {m_2}b}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} - \frac{{{m_2}L}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

$$ = \frac{{{m_1}a + {m_2}b}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

Question: The acceleration ‘a’ of a particle starting from rest varies with time according to relation a= $$\alpha $$t +$$\beta $$. The velocity of the particle after a time ‘t’ will be
Solution:
Given that \[a = \alpha t + \beta \]

\[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \alpha t + \beta \]

\[\int_0^v {dv}  = \int_0^t {\left( {\alpha t + \beta } \right)dt}  = \int_0^t {\alpha t\,dt}  + \int_0^t {\beta dt} \]

\[\left( v \right)_0^v = \alpha \int_0^t {t\,dt}  + \beta \int_0^t {dt} \]

\[ = \alpha \left( {\frac{{{t^2}}}{2}} \right)_0^t + \beta \left( t \right)_0^t\]

\[v = \frac{\alpha }{2}{t^2} + \beta t\]

Question:
An ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process as shown in Figure.

\[\Delta {U_{BC}}\; = {\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1,{\rm{ }}{{\rm{Q}}_{AB}}_\; = {\rm{ }}2{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1\]
\[{W_{AB}}\; = \;{\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1,{\rm{ }}{W_{CA}}\; = {\rm{ }} - 3{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1\]
Heat absorbed by the system during process CA is:
Solution:
$$AB \to {\text{isobaric}}$$

$$BC \to {\text{isochoric}}$$

$$CA \to {\text{not}}\,{\text{defined}}$$

For AB

\[Q = \Delta U + W\]                 (First law of thermodynamics)

\[\Delta U_{AB} = 2 - 5 =  - 3\,kJ/mol\]

For ABC

$$\Delta {U_{ABC}} = \Delta {U_{AB}} + \Delta {U_{BC}}$$

\[ = {\rm{ }} - 3{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }} - 8{\rm{ }}kJ/mol\]

For cyclic process $\Delta U = 0$

\[\Delta {U_{ABC}}+\Delta {U_{CA}} =0\\\Rightarrow \Delta U_{CA}= + 8\,kJ/mol\]

\[\Delta U_{CA} = {\rm{ }}Q_{CA}{\rm{  -  }}W_{CA}\]

\[8{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}Q_{CA}{\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}3\]

\[Q_{CA}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }} + 5{\rm{ kJ/mol}}\]

Question: Two point charges \[ + {q_1}\]and \[ + {q_2}\] separated by distance ‘d’ exert a net force  \[{\overrightarrow F _1}\] on a point charge +q kept on the line joining the two charges at a distance d/4 from \[{q_1}\]. On mutually interchanging the positions of the charges \[{q_1}\] and \[{q_2}\], the third charge +q kept at the same point experiences a net force \[{\overrightarrow F _2}\]. If \[\left| {{{\overrightarrow F }_1}} \right| = 2\left| {{{\overrightarrow F }_2}} \right|\] then the ratio \[{q_1}/{q_2}\] may be
Solution:
Using Coulomb's law of forces, using $$\frac{d}{4}$$ distance from one charge and $$d - \frac{d}{4} = \frac{{3d}}{4}$$ from 2nd charge we find that net force will be difference of two forces as both will be in opposite directions (since the charge q is in between both charges)
\[{F_1} = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{16{q_1}q}}{{{d^2}}} - \frac{{16{q_2}q}}{{9{d^2}}}} \right]\]
\[{F_2} = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}\] \[\left[ {\frac{{16{q_2}q}}{{{d^2}}} - \frac{{16{q_1}q}}{{9{d^2}}}} \right]\]
Setting \[\left| {\overline {{F_1}} } \right| = 2\left| {\overline {{F_2}} } \right|\] and solving we get \[\frac{{{q_1}}}{{{q_2}}} = \frac{{19}}{{11}}\]

Question:
Given the equilibrium constants
\[{\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}^ \oplus } + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - } \rightleftharpoons {\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}_2},\,{{\text{K}}_1} = 3 \times {10^6}\]
\[{\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}_2} + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - } \rightleftharpoons {\text{HgCl}}_3^ - ,{{\text{K}}_2} = 8.9\]
The equilibrium constant for the disproportionation equilibrium
\[2{\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}_2} \rightleftharpoons {\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}^ \oplus } + {\text{HgCl}}_3^ - \] is
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[{\text{a}}{\text{.}}\,{\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}^ \oplus } + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - } \rightleftharpoons {\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}_2}.......{{\text{K}}_1}\]

The equilibrium constant(k) for the reaction, 
\[2{\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}_2} \rightleftharpoons {\text{HgC}}{{\text{l}}^ \oplus } + {\text{HgCl}}_3^ - \]

Question: Two wires of equal cross-section but one made of steel and the other of copper are joined end to end. When at the combination is kept under tension, the elongations in the two wires are found to be equal. What is the ratio of the lengths of the two wires lengths (Given, Y for steel = 2 × 1011 Nm-2, Y for copper =  1.1 × 1011 Nm-2  )
Solution:
As, $$Y = \frac{{stress}}{{strain}}$$

Or      $$strain = \frac{{stress}}{Y}$$

Or      $$\frac{{\Delta L}}{L} = \frac{{stress}}{Y}$$

Since, cross-section are equal and same tension exists in both wires, therefore, the stresses developed are equal. Also, $$\Delta L$$ is given to be the same for both the wires.

$$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,L \propto Y$$

$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{{{L_s}}}{{{L_{{C_u}}}}} = \frac{{{Y_s}}}{{{Y_{{C_u}}}}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{11}}}}{{1.1 \times {{10}^{11}}}} = \frac{{20}}{{11}}$$

Solution:
$\tau = C.\theta = \frac{{\pi \eta {r^4}\theta }}{{2L}} = $Constant
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\pi \eta {r^4}(\theta - {\theta _0})}}{{2l}} = \frac{{\pi \eta {{(r/2)}^4}({\theta _0} - \theta ')}}{{2(l/2)}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{(\theta - {\theta _0})}}{2} = \frac{{{\theta _0}}}{{16}} \Rightarrow {\theta _0} = \frac{8}{9}\theta $

Question: Charges 2q, -q and -q lie at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. The value of E and V at the centroid of the triangle will be
Solution:
In an equilateral triangle distance of centroid from all the vertices is same (say r).
$\therefore V = {V_1} + {V_2} + {V_3}$
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{{q_1}}}{r} + \frac{{{q_2}}}{r} + \frac{{{q_3}}}{r}} \right] = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{2q}}{r} - \frac{q}{r} - \frac{q}{r}} \right] = 0$

But ${E_A} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\frac{{2q}}{{{r^2}}}$ along AO,
${E_B} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\frac{q}{{{r^2}}}$ along OB and
${E_C} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\frac{q}{{{r^2}}}$ along OC.
Obviously, ${E_B} + {E_C}$ will also be in the direction of AO (extended) and hence, ${E_A}$ and $\left( {{E_B} + {E_C}} \right)$ being in same direction will not give zero resultant.

Question: The decreasing order of bond length of C=C bond in the following compounds is:
Solution:
The correct option is III > I > II > IV
When a double bond involve in delocalisation of electrons, they will attain more single bond character. Thus, the double bond involve in delocalisation will have larger bond length compare to the isolated double bond.
In given compounds, compound (I) and (II) shows resonance effect whereas (II) and (IV) shows hyperconjugation effect. Since resonance effect is prior to hyperconjugation, compound (1) and (III) has larger bond length compare to (II) and (IV).
In (III), 3 double bonds are involved in resonance while in (II) two double bonds are involved so the bond length of (III) is the largest one. Also (III) is involved in cyclic resonance which make it more stable.
Comparing (II) and (IV), II has larger bond length than (IV) because it has more α− Hydrogen.

A disc is rotating with an angular velocity ω<sub>0</sub>. A constant retarding torque is applied on it to stop the disc. The angular velocity becomes \[\frac{{{\omega _0}}}{2}\] after n rotations. How many more rotations will it make before coming to rest ?
Solution:
Retarding torque is constant. 

Therefore, angular retardation say $$\alpha $$ will also be constant. Applying

$${\omega ^2} = \omega _0^2 - 2\alpha \theta $$

We get,\[{\left( {\frac{{{\omega _0}}}{2}} \right)^2} = \omega _0^2 - 2\alpha {\theta _1}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} .....\left( 1 \right)\]

and $$0 = {\left( {\frac{{{\omega _0}}}{2}} \right)^2} - 2\alpha {\theta _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$$

Solving Eqs (1) and (2), we get

$${\theta _2} = \frac{{{\theta _1}}}{3}$$

Therefore, the disc will make $$\frac{n}{3}$$ more rotations before coming to rest.

Question: A projectile is fired vertically upwards with an initial velocity u. After an interval of T seconds a second projectile is fired vertically upwards, also with initial velocity u.
Options: They meet at time $t = \frac{u}{g}$ and at a height $\frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} + \frac{{g{T^2}}}{8}$
They meet at time $t = \frac{u}{g} + \frac{T}{2}$ and at a height $\frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} + \frac{{g{T^2}}}{8}$
They meet at time $t = \frac{u}{g} + \frac{T}{2}$ and at a height $\frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} - \frac{{g{T^2}}}{8}$
Solution:
For first projectile, ${h_1} = ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$
For second projectile, ${h_2} = u(t - T) - \frac{1}{2}g{(t - T)^2}$
When both meet i.e. ${h_1} = {h_2}$
$ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2} = u(t - T) - \frac{1}{2}g{(t - T)^2}$
⇒ $uT + \frac{1}{2}g{T^2} = gtT$
⇒ $t = \frac{u}{g} + \frac{T}{2}$
and ${h_1} = u\left( {\frac{u}{g} + \frac{T}{2}} \right) - \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {\frac{u}{2} + \frac{T}{2}} \right)^2}$
$ = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} - \frac{{g{T^2}}}{8}$.

Question: A sound source emits frequency of 180 Hz when moving towards a rigid wall with speed 5 m/s and an observer is moving away from wall with speed 5 m/s.  Both source and observer move on a straight line which is perpendicular to the wall.  The number of beats per second heard by the observer will be   (speed of sound = 355 m/s, source is in between observer and wall):
Solution:
\[{f_{wall}} = f\left[ {\frac{{355}}{{355 - 5}}} \right] = \frac{{355}}{{350}}f\]

\[{f_1} = {f_{obs,\,wall}} = {f_{wall}}\left[ {\frac{{355 - 5}}{{355}}} \right] = f = 180Hz\]

\[{f_{obs,\,source}} = f\left[ {\frac{{355 - 5}}{{355 + 5}}} \right] = \frac{{350}}{{360}}f\]

\[{f_2} = {f_{obs,\,source}} = 175\,Hz\]

Beats\[ = {f_1} - {f_2} = 5Hz\]

Question: The relative density of material of a body is found by weighing it first in air and then in water. If the weight in air is (5.00$ \pm 0.05$) N and weight in water is (4.00$ \pm $0.05) N, then the relative density can be written as:  $\left(\text{relative density}= \frac{{{\text{weight}}\,{\text{in}}\,{\text{air }}}}{{{\text{weight in air }}-{\text{weight in water}}}}\right)$
Solution:
Weight in air $ = (5.00 \pm 0.05)\,N$
Weight in water$ = (4.00 \pm 0.05)\,N$
Loss of weight = $L\pm\Delta L$
$L= 5.00-4.00=1.00$
$\Delta L=0.05+0.05=0.1$$\,\,\,(\because \text{error gets added during subtraction})$            
So, loss of weight in water $ = (1.00 \pm 0.1)\,N$
Now relative density $ = \frac{{{\text{weight}}\,{\text{in}}\,{\text{air }}}}{{{\text{weight }}{\text{loss in water}}}}$
i.e. R.D.$ = \frac{{5.00 \pm 0.05}}{{1.00 \pm 0.1}}$
Now relative density with max permissible error $\rho  \pm \Delta \rho $
$\rho  = \frac{{{w_1}}}{{{w_1} - {w_2}}}$
$\ln \rho  = \ln {w_1} - \ln \left( {{w_1} - {w_2}} \right)$
$\frac{{d\rho }}{\rho } = \frac{{d{w_1}}}{{{w_1}}} - \frac{{d\left( {{w_1} - {w_2}} \right)}}{{{w_1} - {w_2}}}$
$ = \frac{{d{w_1}}}{{{w_1}}} - \frac{{d{w_1}}}{{\left( {{w_1} - {w_2}} \right)}} + \frac{{d{w_2}}}{{{w_1} - {w_2}}}$
$ = \frac{{ - 0.05}}{5} + \frac{{0.05}}{1} + \frac{{0.05}}{1}$
$ =  - 1\%  + 5\%  + 5\% $
$ = 9\% $

Question: A solid sphere of radius R is charged uniformly through out the volume. At what distance from its surface is the electrostatic potential half of the potential at the centre.
Solution:
Potential at centre \[{V_c} = K.\frac{{3Q}}{{2R}}\]
\[{V_1} = K.\frac{Q}{r}\]
\[K.\frac{{3Q}}{{4R}} = \frac{{KQ}}{r}\]
\[r = \frac{{4R}}{3}\]
Distance from surface \[\frac{{4R}}{3} - R = \frac{R}{3}\]

Question: A body of mass ${m_1}$ moving with uniform velocity of 40 m/s collides with another mass ${m_2}$ at rest and then the two together begin to move with uniform velocity of 30 m/s. The ratio of their masses $\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}}$ is
Solution:
Initial momentum of the system = ${m_1} \times 40 + {m_2} \times 0$
Final momentum of the system = $({m_1} + {m_2}) \times 30$
By the law of conservation of momentum
${m_1} \times 40 + {m_2} \times 0 = ({m_1} + {m_2}) \times 30$
$$40{m_1} = 30{m_1} + 30{m_2} \Rightarrow 10{m_1} = 30{m_2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = 3$$

Question: If the earth were to suddenly contract to \[\frac{1}{{{n^{th}}}}\] of its present radius without any change in its mass, the duration of the new day will be nearly:
Solution:
Let the mass and radius of the earth be M and R respectively.

$$\therefore {I_1} = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}$$

Also, time period of rotation of earth, T = 24 h

$$\therefore $$ Angular velocity of rotation of earth, 

$${w_1} = \frac{{2\pi }}{T} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{24}}$$

Radius of earth after it shrinks, $$R' = \frac{R}{n}$$

$$\therefore {I_2} = \frac{2}{5}M{\left( {R'} \right)^2} = \frac{2}{{5{n^2}}}M{R^2} = \frac{{{I_1}}}{{{n^2}}}$$

Let the time period of rotation of earth be, $${T_2}$$ hr

Using conservation of angular momentum; $${I_2}{w_2} = {I_1}{w_1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_1}}}{{{n^2}}} \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{{T_2}}} = {I_1} \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{24}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {T_2} = \frac{{24}}{{{n^2}}}hr$$

Question: A ball of mass 0.2 kg rests on a vertical post of height 5 m. A bullet of mass 0.01 kg, travelling with a velocity v m/s in a horizontal direction, hits the centre of the ball. After the collision, the ball and bullet travel independently. The ball hits the ground at a distance of 20 m and the bullet at a distance of 100 m from the foot of the post. The initial velocity v of the bullet is
Solution:
Time taken by the bullet and ball to strike the ground is,
\[t = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{g}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 5}}{{10}}} = 1s\]
Let v1 and v2 be the velocities of ball and bullet after collision. Then applying
x = vt
We have,\[20 = {v_1} \times 1\]
Or \[{v_1} = 20m/s\]
100 = \[{v_2} \times 1\]
Or \[{v_2} = 100m/s\]
Now, from conservation of linear momentum before and after collision we have,
0.01v = (0.2 \[ \times \] 20) + (0.01 \[ \times \] 100)
On solving, we get v = 500 m/s

Question:
Statement-1: A body of mass \[{m_1}\] collides head on elastically with another stationary body of mass \[{m_2}\]. After the collision, velocity of mass \[{m_2}\] is maximum, when \[{m_1}\]<<\[{m_2}\].

Options: Statement-1 is True, Statement-2 is True, Statement-2 is a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is True, Statement-2 is True, Statement-2 is not a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is True, Statement-2 is False
Statement-1 is False, Statement-2 is False
Solution:
Assertion and Reason are both wrong.

From conservation of linear momentum 

\[{m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2}  = u. {m_1} + 0 .{m_2}\]

\[e = \frac{{{v_2} - {v_1}}}{{u - 0}}\]

For elastic collision, e = 1

\[{v_2} - {v_1} = u \Rightarrow {v_1} = {v_2} - u\]

\[{m_1}u + 0 = {m_1}\left( {{v_2} - u} \right) + {m_2}{v_2}\]

\[2{m_1}u = \left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right){v_2}\]

\[{v_2} = \frac{{2{m_1}u}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]

\[{v_2} = \frac{{2{m_1}u}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {v_2} = \frac{{2u}}{{1 + \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}}}}\]

\[{v_2}\] is maximum, when \[\frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} \to 0\]

i.e., \[{m_1} \gg {m_2}\]

Question: Which of the following hormones stimulates pancreas to secrete enzymes?
Solution: Cholecystokinin (CCK), formerly called pancreozymin, is a digestive hormone released with secretin when food from the stomach reaches the first part of the small intestine (duodenum). Cholecystokinin and pancreozymin were once considered two separate hormones because two distinct actions had been described: the release of enzymes from the pancreas, an action ascribed to pancreozymin; and the contraction of the gallbladder, which forces bile into duodenum, an action ascribed to cholecystokinin. However, today these two actions are recognized as belonging to one enzyme, now known solely as cholecystokinin.

Question: In a buffer solution of a weak acid and its salt with a strong base if the ratio of the  concentrations of the salt to that of the acid is increased ten times, the pH of the buffer
Solution:
Using Henderson’s equation,
\[pH = \log \frac{{[Salt]}}{{[Acid]}} + p{K_a}\]
The ratio of the concentration of salt to acid is increased ten times
\[pH = \log \frac{{10[Salt]}}{{[Acid]}} + p{K_a}\]
\[pH = \log 10 + \log \frac{{[Salt]}}{{[Acid]}} + p{K_a}\]
\[pH = 1 + \log \frac{{[Salt]}}{{[Acid]}} + p{K_a}\]
The pH of the solution will increase by 1.

Question: The correct order of electron affinities of N, O, S and Cl is
Solution:
Generally, electron affinity increases as we move from left to right in a period and decreases as we go down in a group. But electron affinities of second period elements (such as N, O) are less negative as compared to corresponding third period element. This is because of the smallest atomic size of second period elements. Hence, the correct order of electron affinities is
N < O < S < Cl

Question: A gas bubble formed from an explosion under water oscillates with a period T proportional to p<sup>a </sup>d<sup>b </sup>E<sup>c</sup>, where p is pressure, d is the density of water and E is the total energy of explosion. The values of a, b and c are
Solution:
(d) Given, $$T \propto {p^a}{d^b}{E^c}$$

We have $$\left[ {{M^0}{L^0}T} \right] = k{\left[ {M{L^{ - 1}}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^a}{\left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}} \right]^b}{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^c}$$

Where, k is a constant.

On comparing dimensions of similar terms, we have

$$\left[ {{M^0}{L^0}T} \right] = k\left[ {{M^{a + b + c}}{L^{ - a - 3b + 2c}}{T^{ - 2a - 2c}}} \right]$$

On comparing powers of M, we have

$$0 = a + b + c\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( i \right)$$

On comparing powers of L, we have 

$$0 =  - a - 3b + 2c\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$$

On comparing power of T, we have

$$1 =  - 2a - 2c\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( {iii} \right)$$

On solving Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we have

$$a =  - \frac{5}{6},b = \frac{1}{2},c = \frac{1}{3}$$

Question: Half-life of a radioactive substance is 20 min. The time between 20% and 80% decay will be
Two charge $ + \,q$ and $ - \,q$ are situated at a certain distance.  At the point exactly midway between them
Options: Electric field and potential both are zero
Electric field is zero, also potential is not zero
Electric field is not zero, but potential is zero
Neither electric field nor potential is zero
Solution:
At $$O$$, $$E_O \ne 0,V_O = 0$$

$${V_O} = {V_ + } + {V_ - } = \frac{{kq}}{r} + \frac{{k\left( { - q} \right)}}{r} = 0$$

Magnitude of electric field at O due to both the charges is the same.

$$\left| {{E_ + }} \right| = \left| {{E_ - }} \right| = \frac{{kq}}{{{r^2}}}$$

Their directions also are the same (towards -q charge).

Therefore, they get added up.

Net field has a magnitude $${E_O} = \frac{{2kq}}{{{r^2}}}$$ and is directed towards -q charge.

Question: A car A is travelling on a straight level road with a uniform speed of 60$km/h.$It is followed by another car B which is moving with a speed of 70 $km/h.$When the distance between them is 2.5 km, the car B is given a deceleration of 20$km/{h^2}.$After how much time will B catch up with A ?
Solution:
Let car B catches, car A after ‘t’ hour, then
$60t + 2.5 = 70t - \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times {t^2}$
⇒ $10{t^2} - 10t + 2.5 = 0$⇒ ${t^2} - t + 0.25 = 0$
$\therefore $ $t = \frac{{1 \pm \sqrt {1 - 4 \times (0.25)} }}{2} = \frac{1}{2}hr$

Question: The beans are cooked sooner in a pressure cooker, because
Options: boiling point increase with increasing pressure
boiling point decrease with increasing pressure
extra pressure of pressure cooker, softens the beans
internal energy is not lost while cooking in pressure cooker.
Solution:
According to ideal gas equation,

PV = nRT 

At constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to the temperature. The pressure is increased sooner inside the cooker, so the temperature also increases earlier and we know the temperature is directly proportional to the kinetic energy, hence the beans are cooked sooner in a pressure cooker.

 Therefore, the correct answer is boiling point increases while increases pressure.

Question: If velocity $v$, acceleration $A$ and force $F$ are chosen as fundamental quantities, then the dimensional formula of angular momentum in terms of $v,\,A$ and $F$ would be
Solution:
Let $L \propto {v^x}{A^y}{F^z}$ $ \Rightarrow $ $L = k{v^x}{A^y}{F^z}$
Putting the dimensions in the above relation
$[M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}] = k{[L{T^{ - 1}}]^x}{[L{T^{ - 2}}]^y}{[ML{T^{ - 2}}]^z}$
⇒ $[M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}] = k[{M^z}{L^{x + y + z}}{T^{ - x - 2y - 2z}}]$
Comparing the powers of $M,\,L$ and $T$
$z = 1$ …(i)
$x + y + z = 2$ …(ii)
$ - x - 2y - 2z = - 1$ …(iii)
On solving (i), (ii) and (iii) $x = 3,\,y = - 2,\,z = 1$
So dimension of $L$ in terms of $v,\,A$ and $F$
$[L] = [F{v^3}{A^{ - 2}}]$

Question: In the arrangement shown in figure, string and pulley are ideal. \[{m_A} = 2\;{\text{kg}}\] and \[{m_B} = 4\;{\text{kg}}\]. The acceleration of COM of system is
Solution:
$$T-2 g=2 a \rightarrow \text { (i) } 4 g-T=4 a \rightarrow \text { (ii) }$$

From (i) and (ii) $4 g-2 g-2 a=4 a \Rightarrow a=\frac{g}{3}$

$$\mathop  \uparrow \limits_{ + ve} {m_1} = 2\;{\text{kg}}\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} {m_2} = 4\;{\text{kg}}$$

$$ =  - \frac{{2g}}{3} \times \frac{1}{6} =  - \frac{g}{9}$$

Question: The frequency (f) of a stretched string depends upon the tension F (force), length \[l\] of the string and the mass per unit length \[\mu \] of string. The formula for frequency is
Solution:
Let $$f = k{F^a}{l^b}{\mu ^c}$$

$$\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right] = k{\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^a}{\left[ L \right]^b}{\left[ {M{L^{ - 1}}} \right]^c}$$

$$\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right] = k\left[ {{M^{a + c}}{L^{a + b - c}}{T^{ - 2a}}} \right]$$

Comparing the powers

$$a + c = 0,\,\,a + b - c = 0,\, - 2a =  - 1$$

Solving these equations we get $$a =  + \frac{1}{2},\,\,b =  - 1$$ and

$$c = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}$$

$$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} f = k{F^{1/2}}{l^{ - 1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mu ^{ - 1/2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} f = k\frac{1}{l}\sqrt {\frac{F}{\mu }} $$

where k is a dimensionless constant.

Question: A shell is fired from a cannon with velocity v m/s at an angle $\theta $ with the horizontal direction. At the highest point in its path, it explodes into two pieces of equal mass. One of the pieces retraces its path to the cannon, then the speed in m/s of the other piece immediately after the explosion is
Solution:
Shell is fired with velocity v at an angle θ with the horizontal.
So its velocity at the highest point
= horizontal component of velocity =$v\cos \theta $
So momentum of shell before explosion = $mv\cos \theta $

When it breaks into two equal pieces and one piece retraces its path to the cannon, then other part moves with velocity V  (say).

So momentum of two pieces after explosion
                $ = \frac{m}{2}( - v\cos \theta ) + \frac{m}{2}V$
By the law of conservation of momentum
$mv\cos \theta = \frac{{ - m}}{2}v\cos \theta + \frac{m}{2}V$⇒ $V = 3v\cos \theta $

Question: A force $\vec F = \alpha \hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k$ is acting at a point $\vec r = 2\hat i - 6\hat j - 12\hat k$. The value of  \[\alpha \]   for which angular momentum is conserved about origin is
Solution:
For the conservation of angular momentum about origin, the torque about origin will be zero.
By definition, $$\vec \tau  = \vec r \times \vec F$$ 
Here, $\vec r = 2\hat i - 6\hat j - 12\hat k$ 
And $\vec F = \alpha \hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k$ 
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,\,\vec \tau = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 
  2&{ - 6}&{ - 12} \\ 
  \alpha &3&6 
\end{array}} \right| \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \hat i\left( { - 36 + 36} \right) - \hat j\left( {12 + 12\alpha } \right) + \hat k\left( {6 + 6\alpha } \right) \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = - \hat j\left( {12 + 12\alpha } \right) + \hat k\left( {6 + 6\alpha } \right) \\ 
\end{gathered} $ 
But $\vec \tau = 0$ 
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,12 + 12\alpha = 0\,\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\,\alpha = - 1 \\
  {\text{and }}\,\,\,\,6 + 6\alpha = 0\,\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\,\alpha = - 1 \\ 
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The power factor of a series RL circuit is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]. If the frequency of the applied ac is doubled, the power factor becomes
Solution:
Power factor $$\cos \phi  = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{R}{Z};$$

$$\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2} }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} }} \Rightarrow {\omega ^2}{L^2} = {R^2}$$

When the frequency $$\omega $$ is doubled,

$$\cos \phi  = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + 4{\omega ^2}{L^2}} }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + 4{R^2}} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \phi  = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 }}$$

Question: In an a.c. circuit the voltage applied is \[E = {E_0}\sin \omega t.\]  The resulting current in the circuit is \[I = {I_0}\sin \left( {\omega t - \frac{\pi }{2}} \right).\]  The power consumption in the circuit is given by

Question: A rms current of 2.5 A flows in a coil when it is connected in a circuit having A.C. of frequency 50 Hz and power consumed in the coil is 250 W. If the rms p.d. across the coil is 200 V, then the resistance and reactance of the coil are
Solution:
Impedance, $Z = \frac{{\,{\text{voltage}}}}{{{\text{current}}}}$$ = \frac{{200}}{{2.5}} = 80\,\Omega $
Power consumed $ = V \times I\cos \theta = V \times I \times \frac{R}{Z}$
$250 = 200 \times 2.5 \times \frac{R}{Z};\,\,R = \frac{{250 \times Z}}{{200 \times 2.5}}$
$R = \frac{{80}}{2} = 40\,\Omega $
${Z^2} = {R^2} + X_L^2$
where ${X_L}$ is inductive reactance
$6400 = 1600 + X_L^2;$ $ \Rightarrow X_L^2 = 6400 - 1600$
$X_L^2 = 4800$ $ \Rightarrow {X_L} = 40 \times \sqrt 3 $
Resistance is $40\,\Omega $

$${\omega _0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }} \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{{L{\omega _0}^2}} = \frac{1}{{0.2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times {{\left( {4 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}^2}}}$$

$$ = \frac{1}{{32}}\mu F = 0.03125\,\mu F$$

Question: Reducing sugars are:
Solution:
Reducing sugars are carbohydrates that can act as reducing agents because they have a free aldehyde group or a free ketone group. In their cyclic form, this corresponds to having a free hemiacetal or hemiketal group, which can open up to form the free carbonyl.

Let's analyze the given options:

  • Monosaccharides like Glucose, Fructose, and Galactose are all reducing sugars.
  • Disaccharides like Maltose and Lactose are reducing sugars because one of the monosaccharide units has a free hemiacetal group.
  • Sucrose is a disaccharide but is a non-reducing sugar because the anomeric carbons of both glucose and fructose are involved in the glycosidic bond, leaving no free hemiacetal group.
  • Polysaccharides like Starch, Cellulose, and Glycogen are considered non-reducing. Although they have a reducing end, the number of non-reducing ends is overwhelmingly larger, making their reducing property negligible.

Evaluating the options based on this:

  • (a) Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Maltose & Lactose: All of these are reducing sugars.
  • (b) Glucose, Sucrose & Cellulose: Sucrose and Cellulose are non-reducing.
  • (c) Lactose, Starch, Glycogen & Trehalose: Starch, Glycogen, and Trehalose (another non-reducing disaccharide) are non-reducing.

Hence, the group containing only reducing sugars is in option (a).

The correct option is (a) Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Maltose & Lactose.

Question: Why is phloem parenchyma absent in a monocot stem?
Options: Because the function of food storage is performed by water cavities
There is no stored food in monocots
Function of phloem parenchyma is taken over by sieve tubes
Solution:
Phloem parenchyma mainly performs storage of food and helps in lateral conduction.

In many monocot stems (e.g., maize), large water-containing cavities are present in the ground tissue.

Because of this structural adaptation, the food storage role typically performed by phloem parenchyma in dicots is not required in the same way in monocots.

Hence, phloem parenchyma is generally absent in monocot stems.


Now checking options:

(A) Because the function of food storage is performed by water cavities — Accepted explanation in some reference books.

(B) There is no stored food in monocots — Incorrect.

(C) Function taken over by sieve tubes — Incorrect; sieve tubes conduct food, not store it.

(D) None of the above — Not correct if we accept functional explanation.

Therefore, (A) is correct

Question: Ground tissue is differentiated into cortex and pith in

Dicot stem → Ground tissue differentiated into cortex, endodermis, pericycle and central pith.

Monocot root → Has distinct cortex and well-developed central pith.

Monocot leaf → Mesophyll not differentiated into cortex and pith. 

Hence, correct answer is (B)

Question: In the most simple type of canal system in Porifera, which of the following displays the correct direction of water flow?
Solution: The central body cavity of sponges is called spongocoel. Their body is porous. The inhalant pores are called ostia and exhalant pores are known as oscula. The body of a sponge is organized in such a manner that it forms a complex system of pores and canals. This system is called a canal system. Water flows in through the ostia to the spongocoel and comes out to exterior via osculum. The continuous water current flowing through the canal system is very important for the life of a sponge. It brings in food and oxygen and carries away carbon dioxide, nitrogenous wastes and reproductive bodies. Thus the canal system helps the sponge in nutrition, respiration, excretion and reproduction.

Chordates are not vertebrates and vertebrates are not chordates.

General features of reptiles are:
- All reptiles have a backbone, which means they are vertebrates.
- All reptiles produce eggs. Most reptiles lay hard-shelled eggs, but a few give birth to live young.
- All reptiles have scales which are small, hard plates that are made from a protein called keratin.
- Reptiles are ectothermic or cold-blooded, which means they cannot control their own body temperature. They must work with their environment to increase or decrease their body temperature. That’s why most reptiles move from place to place by crawling in order to keep the body temperature warm.
- Except for crocodilians, which have a four-chambered heart, all reptiles have a three-chambered heart consisting of two atria and one ventricle. The chamber called the right atrium receives deoxygenated blood returning from the body tissues.

Question: One mole of diatomic ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process ABC as shown in figure. The process BC is adiabatic. The temperature at A, B and C are 400K, 800K and 600K respectively. Choose the correct statement:

The changes in internal energy in the process AB is 350 R.

Solution:
Change in internal energy is given as
$\Delta U = \frac{{nR\Delta T}}{{\gamma - 1}} = \frac{{nR\Delta T}}{{0.4}}$
(for diatomic gas $\gamma = 1.4$)
$ \Rightarrow \Delta {U_{CA}} = \frac{R}{{0.4}}\left( { - 200} \right) = - 500R$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta {U_{AB}} = \frac{R}{{0.4}}\left( {400} \right) = 1000R$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta {U_{BC}} = \frac{R}{{0.4}}\left( { - 200} \right) = - 500R$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{{16}}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{{16 - 1}}{{16}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{{15R}}{{16}} \Rightarrow \lambda  = \frac{{16}}{{15R}}$$

Question:
Read the following statements:
A. Pollination is a broadly utilitarian service of ecosystem.
B. Philosophically or spiritually, we need to realise that every species has an intrinsic value, even if it may not be of current or any economic value to us.
Options: Both A and B are correct
Both A and B are incorrect
Solution:
Pollination is an important ecosystem service that provides direct benefits to humans, so statement A is correct.
Biodiversity also has intrinsic value beyond economic benefits, so statement B is correct

Question: Which of the following best describes the term species diversity?
Options: The variety of alleles found within a group of organisms
The variety of species found in a particular area
The variety of ecosystems found in a certain geographical location
The variety of genes found in an organism
Solution: Species diversity refers to the number of different species and their relative abundance in a given area or ecosystem. It focuses on how many kinds of species (plants, animals, microorganisms) live in a particular region.

Question: Find the wrongly matched pair.
Options: Endemism-Species confined to one region and not found anywhere else
Lungs of the planet-Amazon rain forest
In-situ conservation-conservation away from natural habitat
Solution: The pair in option d is incorrectly matched. In-situ conservation is the conservation at the site where the organism is naturally found. It is also called on-site conservation.

Question:
Biodiversity regions are legally protected as
A. Biosphere reserves
B. Hotspots
C. National parks
D. Sacred groves
E. Wildlife sanctuaries
Options: A, B, C, D and E
Solution: In India, ecologically unique and biodiversity-rich regions are legally conserved as biosphere reserves, national parks, and wildlife sanctuaries. India has 14 biosphere reserves, 448 wildlife sanctuaries and 90 national parks in protected areas. Sacred groves are the natural forest areas important to a particular culture. Hotspots are regions with high biodiversity and endemic species.

Question:
Assertion: Bioprospecting means exploring molecular genetics and species-level diversity for products of economic importance.
Reason: The broadly utilitarian argument says that biodiversity plays a major role in many ecosystem services that nature provides.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Bioprospecting is defined as systematic study of new sources of chemical compounds, genes, microorganisms, macroorganisms, and other value-added products from nature. The broadly utilitarian lays emphasis on oxygen produced by photosynthesis and plant reproduction by pollinator species.

Question: When there are situations where an animal or plant is endangered or threatened and needs urgent measures to save it from extinction, which is the desirable approach?
Solution: When there are situations where an animal or plant is endangered or threatened and needs urgent measures to save it from extinction, ex-situ (off-site) conservation is the most desirable approach.

Question: Threatened animals and plants are taken out from their natural habitat and placed in a special setting where they can be protected and given special care, comes under______.
Solution: To protect the species from being extinct several measures and many practices are being done. Out of those practices, there are in-situ and ex-situ conservation methods. The in-situ method is that in which we conserve species in their natural habitat without any external interference whereas, under ex-situ conservation, threatened or endangered animals and plants are taken out from this natural habitat and placed in a special setting where they can be protected and given special care.

Question: Select the wrong statement:
Options: Steller's Sea Cow and passenger pigeon got extinct  due to over exploitation by man
Lantana and Eichornia are invasive weed species in India
Species diversity increases as we move away from  the equator towards the poles
The historic convention on biological diversity was held in 1992
Solution: The diversity of species decreases as we move away from the equator towards the pole. The equator covers the tropical area which harbours a large number of species but in the poles, the temperature decreases thereby decreasing the diversity of species. A slight modification in the polar area makes it difficult for the species to adapt and survive.

Question:
What is so special about tropics that might account for their greater biological diversity?
(i) Unlike temperate regions subjected to frequent glaciations in the past, tropical latitudes have remained relatively undisturbed for millions of years and thus, had a long evolutionary time for species diversification.
(ii) Temperate environments, unlike tropical ones, are less seasonal, relatively more constant and predictable. Such constant environments promote niche specialization and lead to a greater species diversity.
(iii) There is more solar energy available in the tropics, which contributes to higher productivity; this in turn might contribute indirectly to greater diversity.
Solution: Tropics hold the greatest biodiversity because of constant rainfall and a favourable temperature. Unlike temperate regions subjected to periodic glaciations in the past, tropical latitudes have remained relatively uninterrupted for millions of years and thus, had a long evolutionary time for species diversification. There is more solar energy available in the tropics, which contributes to more productivity; this in turn might contribute indirectly to significant diversity.

Question:
Read the given statements and select the correct options:
Statement I: Sacred groves are examples of in situ conservation of biodiversity.
Statement II: Zoological parks, botanical gardens and wildlife safari parks are other examples of in situ conservation.
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Solution: In-situ conservation means the conservation of a species in its natural habitat and the maintenance and recovery of a viable population of species in their original place. In many cultures, tracts of forest were set aside, and all the trees and wildlife within were venerated and given total protection. Such sacred groves are found in Khasi and Jaintia Hills in Meghalaya, Aravalli Hills of Rajasthan, Western Ghat regions of Karnataka and Maharashtra and the Sarguja, Chanda and Bastar areas of Madhya Pradesh. Sacred grooves are examples of in situ conservation.  In ex situ conservation, the threatened animals and plants are taken out from their natural habitat and placed in special settings where they can be protected and given special care. Zoological parks, botanical gardens and wildlife safari parks serve this purpose.

Question:
Following are excerpts from a forest journey narrative. Read them and find the correct classification of these excerpts based on the justification provided for conservation of biodiversity:
Excerpt I: My trip through lush green forests was always exciting. I walked through thick woods, watched spring flowers, and experienced the calmness and beauty of nature in all its diverse creations which a person living in a metropolitan city can only dream of unless he visits a forest.
Excerpt II: The tribals living near the forest are not only friendly to the outsiders but I always admire the way they worship trees and animals.
Excerpt III: Forests offer countless benefits to us such as fruits, firewood, dyes, perfumes, medicines, and so on and we must be grateful and aware of their conservation.
Options: I and II are broadly utilitarian arguments while III is a narrowly utilitarian
I is a narrowly utilitarian argument while II and III are broadly utilitarian
I is broadly utilitarian, II is ethical and III is a narrowly utilitarian argument
I is narrowly utilitarian, II and III are broadly utilitarian arguments
Solution: Excerpt I is a broadly utilitarian argument that biodiversity plays a major role in many ecosystem services that nature provides. II is an ethical argument for conserving biodiversity that relates to what we owe to millions of plants, animals, and microbe species with whom we share this planet. III is a narrowly utilitarian argument that humans derive countless direct economic benefits from nature.

Question:
Assertion: The ‘biodiversity hotspots’ have very high levels of species richness and high degree of endemism.
Reason: The hotspots are also regions of accelerated habitat loss.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: For maximum protection certain ‘biodiversity hotspots’ regions with very high levels of species richness and high degree of endemism have been identified. Initially 25 biodiversity hotspots were identified but subsequently nine more have been added to the list, bringing the total number of biodiversity hotspots in the world to 34. These hotspots are also regions of accelerated habitat loss. So, Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Question: The regions in India which have the richest biodiversity are:
Options: Gangetic plains and both the eastern and western coastal regions
Solution: The regions in India which are richest in terms of biodiversity are the Western Ghats (4%) and the Himalayas.

Question: What does the shape of the given age pyramids reflects about the growth status of the related population?
Solution:
Triangular age pyramid has high proportion of pre-reproductive individuals, moderate number of reproductive individuals and fewer post-reproductive individuals. It represents young or rapidly growing population.
In urn-shaped age pyramid, the number of pre-reproductive individuals is higher than the number of pre-reproductive individuals. It represents declining or diminishing population.
In bell-shaped age pyramid, the number of pre-reproductive and reproductive individuals is almost equal. Post reproductive individuals are comparatively fewer. It represents stable or stationary population where growth rate is nearly zero.

Question: Rules of conduct that may be used to regulate our activities in relation to the biological world is called
Solution: Rules of conduct that may be used to regulate our activities in relation to the biological world is called Bioethics. With the progress of biotechnology issues, Bioethics is receiving attention.

Question:
In a comparative analogy between the airplane and ecosystem (the ‘rivet popper hypothesis) used by Stanford ecologist Paul Ehrlich, find out the correct matching.
Column IColumn II

b.Airplane2.Species
c.Popping a rivet3.Key species
d.Rivets on the wings4.Proper functioning of ecosystem
e.Flight safety5.A species to become extinct
Solution: The ‘rivet popper hypothesis was used by Stanford ecologist Paul Ehrlich in a comparative analogy between the airplane and the ecosystem. Rivet represents the species, airplane represents the ecosystem, popping a rivet means that a species is being extinct, rivets on the wings mean key species and lastly, flight safety means proper functioning of the ecosystem.

Question: The death of native plants with the introduction of invasive weed species is an example of
Solution: The growth of invasive weed species in an ecosystem threatens the survival of the native plant species growing in that ecosystem. This is an example of alien species invasion, where the introduction of a new species into an ecosystem preys upon and kills the native species or uses up the resources necessary for the growth of the native organisms.

Question:
(i) Sacred groves
(ii) Hot spots
(iii) National park
(iv) Wildlife safari park
(v) Seed bank
(vi) Biosphere reserve
(a) i, ii, iii, vi are the example of in-situ conservation
(b) iv, v are the example of ex-situ conservation
(c) ii, iv, v are the example of ex-situ conservation
(d) i, ii, iii, iv, vi are the example of in-situ conservation
Choose the correct option.
Solution:
There are two approaches for the conservation of biodiversity-

1. In-situ conservation- On-site conservation i.e, threatened plants and animals are protected in their natural habitats. For example- Sacred groves, hot spots, national parks, biosphere reserves.

2. Ex-situ conservation- Off-site conservation i.e., threatened plant and animal species are taken out from their natural habitat and kept in special areas for their protection is known as ex-situ conservation. For example- Wildlife safari park, seed bank, etc.

Question: Extensive human interference can lead to
Options: Co-extinction of parasites along with extinction of their host species
Solution: Extensive human interference can lead to complete destruction of natural habitats or the breaking up of a large, continuous habitat into smaller. isolated patches or fragments. This destruction of habitat can lead to extinction of the species living in these habitats. If there are other organisms (parasites) dependent on the organisms which became extinct, then these parasites will get extinct too along with them. Therefore, extensive human activities can lead to loss and fragmentation habitat, extinction of species and co-extinction of other species dependent on the former species that became extinct due to loss of habitat.

Question:
Which of the statements about "Denaturation" given below is correct?
(1) Denaturation of proteins causes loss of secondary and tertiary structure of the proteins.
(2) Denaturation leads to the conversion of a double-strand of DNA into a single strand.
(3) Denaturation affects primary structure which gets distorted.
Solution: Denaturation does not change the primary structure of the protein.

Question: Which of the following options gives pairs of ex situ conservation sites?
Solution: Ex situ conservation refers to the protection of animals and plants outside of their natural habitat. Zoos, safari parks and botanical gardens are examples of ex situ conservation. However, besides keeping animals in enclosures outside of their natural habitats, ex situ conservation also includes protection of genetic diversity through artificial means. This implies, preserving the embryos, seeds or gametes of endangered plants and animals in extremely low temperatures (cryopreservation). This is done in order to prevent these species from extinction. Such places are known as gene banks or seed banks. These seeds, gametes or embryos can then be made to grow into a new plant inside the lab (in vitro).

Question: The preservation of gametes of threatened plant or animal species is done in order to
Options: Preserve species richness of biodiversity hotspots
Reintroduce extinct animals in the ecosystem
Solution: Genes of endangered plants can be preserved by storing their seeds/gametes in very low temperatures (cryopreservation), to prevent these species from extinction. Such places are known as seed banks. These seeds can then be made to grow into a new plant inside the lab (in vitro).

Question: The Stellar’s sea cow has become extinct as a result of:
Solution: Over-exploitation by humans has led to the extinction of the Stellar’s sea cow.

Question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
Options: Insulin used for diabetic patients was earlier extracted from pancreas of slaughtered cattle and pigs which was more efficient than the genetically engineered insulin.
PCR technique is applied to detect HIV in suspected AIDS patients and to detect mutations in genes in suspected cancer patients
Bone marrow transplantation requires periodic infusion of genetically engineered lymphocytes in ADA deficient patients.
Bioremediation is one of the applications of biotechnology.
Solution: Statement (a) is incorrect.

Question:
What is common to the following techniques?
(i) In vitro fertilization,
(ii) Cryopreservation and,
(iii) Tissue culture
Options: All are in situ conservation methods
All are ex situ conservation methods
All require ultramodern equipment and large space
All are methods of conservation of extinct organisms.
Solution: Ex-situ conservation methods include protective maintenance of threatened species in zoological parks and botanical gardens, in vitro fertilisation, tissue culture propagation, and cryopreservation of gametes.

Question: Which of the following is an example of co-extinction?
Options: Death of climbers and creepers due to deforestation
Death of cichlid fishes due to introduction of the Nile perch fish
Death of marine fishes due to extensive human activities
Death of parasitic insects due to killing of the host fish species
Solution: Co-extinction refers to the extinction of one organism leading to the extinction of another organism that is dependent on the former for survival. Parasites living inside a host fish body is dependent on the host for food and survival. Therefore, death of the host fish species will lead to simultaneous extinction of the parasites dependent on it.

Question: Which of the following is NOT a means of ex situ conservation?
Solution: Ex situ conservation refers to the protection of animals and plants outside of their natural habitat. Zoos, safari parks and botanical gardens are examples of ex situ conservation. However, sacred groves are tracts of natural forests that are protected by locals who worship those forests. Thus, sacred groves are a means of in situ conservation of biodiversity, where animals and plants are protected within their natural habitat.

Question: Endemic species are always at a higher risk of extinction than others because
Options: They are mostly preys that are always under the threat of predators
They are exclusively found in only one place in the world
They are weaker in comparison to other non-endemic species
They are dangerous and hence cannot be kept protected in captivity
Solution: Endemic species are those that are found exclusively in one region in the entire world. If this region is destroyed, then these species become extinct since they don’t exist anywhere else in the world. That is why endemic species always face a higher risk of extinction than others.

Question:

The most abundant organism that lives in hydrothermal vents are chemolithotrophs that use reduced chemical species, most often sulfur, as sources of energy to reduce carbon dioxide to organic carbon. Chemolithotrophs are prokaryotes with a modified cell wall.

Question: The primitive prokaryotes responsible for the production of biogas from the dung of ruminant animals, include the
Solution: Methanogens are a group of obligate anaerobic ancient and primitive bacteria. They are involved in methanogenesis and produce methane gas in cattles.

Question: The five kingdoms of Whittaker arranged in an evolutionary manner are
Solution: Whittaker proposed an elaborate five kingdom classification, which on an evolutionary scale, can be arranged in the following chronological order - Kingdom Monera, Kingdom Protista, Kingdom Fungi, Kingdom Plantae and Kingdom Animalia. The main criteria of the five kingdom classification were cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition and reproduction, and phylogenetic relationships.

Question: _________ causes sleeping sickness.
Solution: Trypanosoma brucei causes sleeping sickness. It is a flagellated protozoans. Early symptoms include fever, headaches, joint pains and itching.

Question: Mycoplasma is classified under which of the following kingdoms?
Solution: Mycoplasma or mollicutes are the simplest and the smallest of the free-living prokaryotes classified under kingdom Monera. Mycoplasma is an organism that completely lacks a cell wall. They are the smallest living cells known and can survive without oxygen. Many Mycoplasmas are pathogenic in animals and plants.

Question: Bovine spongiform encephalopathy is a bovine disease. To which of the following human diseases it is related?
Solution: Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (BSE) is a fatal brain disease known to exists in beef and other dairy cattle in UK, also known as mad cow disease. It is believed to be caused by prions. Creutzfeldt-Jacob Disease (CJD) is a slow degenerative disease among human affecting central nervous system with dysfunction and degeneration of the brain. Some scientists have suggested that a few people in Britain might have contracted CJD by eating BSE-infected beef.

Question: The relation between algae and fungi in a lichen is
Solution: The relation between algae and fungi in a lichen is symbiosis. In lichen fungi is for water intake and algae is photosynthetic and prepares food.

Question: A free-living, nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria which can also form a symbiotic association with the water fern Azolla is
Solution: Anabaena is a free-living, nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria that can form a symbiotic association with water fern Azolla.

Question: Bacteria can be classified into two groups on the basis of the differences in cell envelopes and the manner in which they respond to staining procedure as
Solution: The grouping of bacteria is based upon reaction of bacteria to Gram’s stain (Christian Gram, 1884). Bacteria are first stained with a weakly alkaline solution of crystal violet and then treated with 0.5% iodine solution. This is followed by washing with water and then with alcohol or acetone. Bacteria which retain blue or purple colour are known as Gram (+) bacteria (e. g., Bacillus subtilis). Bacteria which do not retain any stain and become colourless are called Gram (-) bacteria (e.g., Escherichia coli). Washing of the stain in Gram (-ve) bacteria is due to the high lipid content of their cell wall which gets dissolved in organic solvents like acetone.

Question: Which of the following parasites is classified as a non-living creature outside the cell of a living organism, but becomes living on entering it?
Solution: Bacteriophages are viruses and viruses are not living things. Viruses are complicated assemblies of molecules, including proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and carbohydrates, but on their own they can do nothing until they enter a living cell. Without cells, viruses would not be able to multiply. Therefore, viruses are not living things.

Question: Select the correct statement regarding heterocysts.
Options: These are present in some filamentous cyanobacteria such as Nostoc and Anabaena.
These cells are specialized to perform N<sub>2</sub>-fixation.
Solution: Heterocyst is a large-sized pale coloured thick-walled cell which occurs in terminal, intercalary or lateral position in filamentous cyanobacteria, e.g., Nostoc. The thick wall is impermeable to oxygen but permeable to nitrogen. Mucilage sheath is absent. Photosystem II is absent. Thylakoids lack phycobilisomes. Therefore, photosynthesis is absent but cyclic photophosphorylation occurs. Heterocyst is dependent for its nourishment on adjacent vegetative cells. Question: Read the following statements and find out the incorrect statement.

Fats and oils are differentiated on the basis of melting points.
Solution:
• The macromolecules from the cytoplasm and organelles become the acid-insoluble fraction. This statement is correct.
  • Monoglycerides, diglycerides, and triglycerides are lipids having both glycerol and fatty acids.  This statement is correct.

  • Fats are more saturated than oil, so they have a higher melting point. This statement is correct.

Question:
RNA interference leads to resistance in plants by (i)_____ specific (ii) ____ resulting in
(iii) ______ of protein synthesis.
Solution:
RNA interference (RNAi) in plants works like this:
1. Mechanism: RNAi involves small RNA molecules (siRNA or miRNA) that target specific mRNA sequences in the cell.
2. Action: These small RNAs guide the cell's machinery to degrade the target mRNA.
3. Effect: Since the mRNA is degraded, it cannot be translated into protein, leading to inhibition of protein synthesis.
Matching this with the options:
(i) specific → yes, RNAi is specific.
(ii) mRNA → yes, it targets mRNA, not DNA or tRNA.
(iii) inhibition → yes, protein synthesis is inhibited.

Question:
Which of the following is/are correct about Adenosine Deaminase (ADA) deficiency?
I. In the absence of adenosine deaminase enzyme, purine metabolism is disturbed and T-lymphocytes fails to function
II. ADA deficiency is caused by the deletion of the gene for ADA
III. In some cases, it can be cured by bone marrow transplantation and enzyme replacement therapy. But in both approaches, the patients are not completely cured
IV. If genes isolated from the bone marrow cells that produce ADA is introduced into target cells at early embryonic stages, it can be a possible cure for ADA deficiency
Which of the above statements are correct?
Solution:
Adenosine deaminase enzyme is very important for the immune system to function. In the absence of adenosine deaminase enzyme, purine metabolism is disturbed and T-lymphocytes fail to function. ADA deficiency can lead to Severe combined Immune Deficiency (SCID)
SCID is caused due to a defect in the genes for the enzyme adenosine deaminase. The genetic diseases that are being investigated for gene therapy range from sickle-cell anaemia to Severe Combined Immuno Deficiency (SCID). In some children, ADA deficiency can be cured by bone marrow transplantation.
However, in others, it can be treated by enzyme replacement therapy, in which functional ADA is given to the patient by injection. But in both approaches, the patients are not completely cured. For permanent cure, genes need to be isolated from the bone marrow cells producing ADA and these genes need to be introduced to target cells at early early embryonic stages. This is known as embryonic gene therapy.

Question:
Which of the following statements are false?
I. Insulin for curing diabetes, used to be extracted from the pancreas of slaughtered pig and cattle
II. Animal insulin is slightly different from the human insulin
III. Animal insulin causes some undesirable side effects such as allergy
IV. Bacteria cannot be made to synthesise insulin from its gene because of the presence of introns
Choose the correct option
Solution: Earlier, insulin was extracted from the pancreas of slaughtered cattle and pigs but some patients began developing allergies. Bacteria can not be made to synthesize insulin from the human gene because of the presence of introns. The human gene contains both noncoding introns and a coding sequence called exons. Bacteria do not possess enzymes for removing non-coding sequences called introns from mRNA formed after transcription.

Question: For effective treatment of the disease, early diagnosis and understanding its pathophysiology is very important. Which of the following molecular diagnostic techniques is very useful for early detection?
Solution: ELISA can be used for early detection of infection either by detecting the presence of pathogenic antigen or by detecting the antibodies synthesized against the pathogen. In the hybridization technique, an ssDNA/ssRNA tagged with a radioactive molecule (probe) is allowed to hybridize its complementary DNA in a clone of cells followed by detection using autoradiography. It is used to find a mutated gene. The western blotting technique is used to detect a specific protein molecule among a mixture of proteins. Southern blotting is used to detect a specific DNA sequence in the given sample and can be detected prior to antibody formation. One can detect the presence of pathogenic DNA/RNA.

Question:
Biopatents are

(ii) Right to use biological entities
(iii) Right to use products
(iv) Right to use process

A patent is a right granted by a government to an inventor to prevent others from the commercial use of his invention. When patents are granted for biological entities and for products derived from them, these are called biopatents.
Biopatents grant:
- Right to use the invention
- Right to use biological entities
- Right to use products
- Right to use the process
Thus the correct option is (d).

Question:
Assertion: Bt toxin gene has been cloned from the bacteria and been expressed in plants to provide resistance to insects without the need for insecticides.
Reason: The Bt toxin protein exists as inactive protoxins, but once an insect ingests the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the gut which solubilises the crystals.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Some strains of Bacillus thuringiensis produce proteins that kill certain insects such as lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles) and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms protein crystals during a particular phase of their growth. These crystals contain a toxic insecticidal protein. Actually, the Bt toxin protein exists as inactive protoxins but once an insect ingests the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the gut which solubilised the crystals. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause cell swelling and lysis and eventually cause death of the insect. So, Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Question: ADA deficiency causes -
Options: reduction in the number of RBCs
Solution: The correct answer is (a). Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency in humans results in combined immunodeficiency (SCID). This immunodeficiency is associated with severe disturbances in purine metabolism that are thought to mediate lymphotoxicity. ADA deficiency is one of the most severe of the immunodeficiencies in humans and is associated with a severe depletion of all three major categories of lymphocytes, T cells, B cells, and NK cells. ADA deficiency is the second-most prevalent form of severe combined immunodeficiency disease (SCID), accounting for approximately 20% of the group.

Question: Choose wrong statement with respect to RNA interference
Options: RNAi takes place in all eukaryotic organisms as a method of cellular defense.
RNAi involves silencing of a specific m-RNA due to a complementary ds DNA.
Source of complementary RNA may be mobile genetic elements (Transposons) that replicate via an RNA intermediate.
Introduction of specific DNA fragment can produce specific ds RNA which can cause RNA interference in the target cell of the host.
Solution: RNAi(RNA interference) takes place in all eukaryotic organisms as a method of cellular defense. This method involves the silencing of a specific mRNA due to a dsRNA and not a dsDNA molecule that binds and prevents translation of the mRNA (silencing). Hence, the statement in option (b) is wrong. The source of this complementary RNA could be from infection by viruses having RNA genomes or mobile genetic elements (transposons) that replicate via an RNA intermediate. Deliberate introduction of a DNA fragment synthesizing the desired ds RNA in the host cell can cause RNA interference in the target cells of the host.

Solution: When a gene or genes have been artificially inserted in an organism, is known as transgenic organism.

Solution: Bacterial cells can be genetically modified so that they have the gene for producing human insulin. As these modified bacteria grow, they produce human insulin.

Question: What is antisense technology?
Options: When a piece of RNA that is complementary in the sequence is used to stop expression of a specific gene
A cell displaying a foreign antigen used for the synthesis of antigens
Production of somaclonal variants in tissue cultures
Solution: Antisense technology is a process by which a piece of RNA that is complementary in the sequence is used to stop expression of a specific gene. Antisense therapy is a form of treatment for genetic disorders or infections. When the genetic sequence of a particular gene is known to be causative of a particular disease, it is possible to synthesize a strand of nucleic acid that will bind to the messenger RNA produced by that gene and inactivate it, effectively turning that gene "off". This is because mRNA has to be single-stranded for it to be translated. Alternatively, the strand might be targeted to bind a splicing site on pre-mRNA and modify the exon content of an mRNA.

Question:
An enzyme is crucial for the immune system to function properly, but the gene producing this enzyme has been found to be deleted, leading to a severely compromised immune system. After much discussion, a team of doctors concluded that this disorder can be cured permanently by

(II) SiRNA approach.
(III) introducing bone marrow cells producing ADA into cells at early embryonic stages
(IV) Inserting the functional gene using retroviruses.
(V) Periodic infusion of genetically engineered lymphocytes having functional ADA cDNA.
Solution: The only cure currently and routinely available for SCID is bone marrow transplant, specifically introducing bone marrow cells producing ADA into cells at early embryonic stages, which provides a new immune system to the patient. Gene therapy treatment of SCID has also been successful in clinical trials, but not without complications. In this, periodic infusion of genetically engineered lymphocytes having functional ADA cDNA, shows a great result for the cure of SCID.

Question: Nucleus of a donor embryo cell/somatic cell is transferred to an enucleated egg cell. Then after the formation of organism, what shall be true?

The organism will have extra-nuclear genes of the recipient cell
The organism will have extra-nuclear genes of both the donor and recipient cellst
The organism will have nuclear genes of the recipient cell
Solution: The organism will have extra-nuclear genes of the recipient cell. Since, the recipient cell has already been enucleated (its nucleus is removed), the organism developing from it would have the nuclear genes of donor cell.

Question: What might be an advantage of beginning gene therapy prior to birth? Gene therapy can be initiated prior to birth too. An advantage of this technique is that

The body would not reject it as it has not yet recognised self
The cells being extremely young are more receptive of gene therapy
Solution: Gene therapy before birth targets fetal cells, which are rapidly dividing and undifferentiated. Such young cells are more receptive to taking up new genes, making the therapy more effective and possibly preventing disease before symptoms appear.

Question:
With reference to recombinant proteins, consider the following statements:
I. Recombinant proteins are synthesised through laboratory techniques that involve manipulation of genetic material.
II. The genetic information for such proteins is carried on artificially constructed recombinant DNA molecules.
III. Recombinant proteins find applications in multiple sectors, including improvements in agricultural practices and productivity.
How many of the statements given above are incorrect?
Solution:
Recombinant Proteins:
Recombinant proteins are proteins that are produced by inserting the gene encoding the protein into a host organism using laboratory techniques. This process involves manipulation of genetic material, such as cutting and joining DNA fragments, so statement I is correct.
Role of Recombinant DNA:
The gene of interest is carried on an artificially constructed recombinant DNA (rDNA) molecule, which is introduced into a host cell (like bacteria, yeast, or mammalian cells). The host then expresses the protein encoded by this gene. Hence, statement II is correct.
Applications:
Recombinant proteins have multiple applications:
● Medicine: insulin, vaccines, monoclonal antibodies.
● Agriculture: Bt toxin in Bt-cotton for pest resistance.
● Industry: enzymes for detergents, food processing.
Therefore, all three statements are correct, and none are incorrect.

Question: Choose the correct pair from the following.

Exonucleases - Make cuts at specific positions within DNA
Ligases - Join the two DNA molecules
Polymerases - Break the DNA into fragments

Question:
Assertion: In rDNA technology, the restriction enzymes that produce sticky ends are commonly used.
Reason: Sticky ends facilitate the action of enzyme DNA ligase.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.

Question: Select the incorrect statement

Insertional inactivation of β-galactosidase leads to colourless colonies
Insertional inactivation of β-galactosidase leads to blue color colonies
In insertional inactivation, the rDNA is inserted within the coding sequence of an enzyme β-galactosidase
Solution:
Selection of recombinants due to inactivation of antibiotics is a cumbersome procedure because it requires simultaneous plating on two plates having different antibiotics. Therefore, alternative
selectable markers have been developed which differentiate recombinants from non-recombinants on the basis of their ability to produce colour in the presence of a chromogenic substrate. In this, a recombinant DNA is inserted within the coding sequence of an enzyme, β-galactosidase. This results in the inactivation of the gene for synthesis of this enzyme, which is referred to as insertional inactivation. The presence of a chromogenic substrate gives blue coloured colonies if the plasmid in the bacteria does not have an insert. Presence of insert results into insertional inactivation of the β-galactosidase gene and the colonies do not produce any colour, these are identified as recombinant colonies
